Telco Customer Churn — Predicting Churn and Monthly Charges

End-to-end ML analysis: EDA, feature engineering, model ladder, and honest evaluation

Author

Matej

Published

July 2, 2026

1. Introduction

This report works through a full, leakage-safe machine learning pipeline on the Telco Customer Churn dataset (7,043 customers, 21 columns from a US telecom provider), answering two real-world questions:

  • Classification — will a given customer churn (cancel their service)? Target: Churn.
  • Regression — how much is a customer likely to be billed per month, i.e. what is their MonthlyCharges? Framed as: given a customer’s plan and service mix, what monthly charge would we expect — useful for pricing sanity checks and flagging accounts that are billed unusually high or low for their service mix.

A single fixed random seed is used throughout for reproducibility.

Code 
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

RANDOM_STATE = 42
np.random.seed(RANDOM_STATE)

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid")
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", 50)

2. Load & inspect the data

Code 
df_raw = pd.read_csv("../data/WA_Fn-UseC_-Telco-Customer-Churn.csv")
print(df_raw.shape)
df_raw.head(3)
(7043, 21)
customerID gender SeniorCitizen Partner Dependents tenure PhoneService MultipleLines InternetService OnlineSecurity OnlineBackup DeviceProtection TechSupport StreamingTV StreamingMovies Contract PaperlessBilling PaymentMethod MonthlyCharges TotalCharges Churn
0 7590-VHVEG Female 0 Yes No 1 No No phone service DSL No Yes No No No No Month-to-month Yes Electronic check 29.85 29.85 No
1 5575-GNVDE Male 0 No No 34 Yes No DSL Yes No Yes No No No One year No Mailed check 56.95 1889.5 No
2 3668-QPYBK Male 0 No No 2 Yes No DSL Yes Yes No No No No Month-to-month Yes Mailed check 53.85 108.15 Yes
Code 
df_raw.dtypes
customerID           object
gender               object
SeniorCitizen         int64
Partner              object
Dependents           object
tenure                int64
PhoneService         object
MultipleLines        object
InternetService      object
OnlineSecurity       object
OnlineBackup         object
DeviceProtection     object
TechSupport          object
StreamingTV          object
StreamingMovies      object
Contract             object
PaperlessBilling     object
PaymentMethod        object
MonthlyCharges      float64
TotalCharges         object
Churn                object
dtype: object

Quirks found on inspection:

  • customerID is a unique identifier with no predictive value — dropped before modeling.
  • TotalCharges is loaded as object (string), not numeric. This is because 11 customers with tenure == 0 (brand-new customers) have a blank string " " instead of a number — pandas falls back to object dtype for the whole column.
  • SeniorCitizen is encoded as 0/1 (already numeric) while most other binary features (Partner, Dependents, PhoneService, …) are encoded as "Yes"/"No" strings.
  • Several categorical columns use a 3-way encoding (e.g. "No", "Yes", "No internet service") which is really “No” in disguise for customers without internet service at all.
Code 
df_raw["TotalCharges"] = pd.to_numeric(df_raw["TotalCharges"], errors="coerce")
print(f"Rows with missing TotalCharges: {df_raw['TotalCharges'].isna().sum()}")
df_raw.loc[df_raw["TotalCharges"].isna(), ["tenure", "MonthlyCharges", "TotalCharges"]]
Rows with missing TotalCharges: 11
tenure MonthlyCharges TotalCharges
488 0 52.55 NaN
753 0 20.25 NaN
936 0 80.85 NaN
1082 0 25.75 NaN
1340 0 56.05 NaN
3331 0 19.85 NaN
3826 0 25.35 NaN
4380 0 20.00 NaN
5218 0 19.70 NaN
6670 0 73.35 NaN
6754 0 61.90 NaN

As suspected, every missing TotalCharges value belongs to a customer with tenure == 0 — they haven’t been billed yet, so there is nothing to fill in from a “true” value. We treat this as missing-not-at-random but explainable: it isn’t noise, it’s structural (new customer).

Code 
df_raw.isna().sum().loc[lambda s: s > 0]
TotalCharges    11
dtype: int64

3. Cleaning

Strategy for the 11 missing TotalCharges rows: since TotalCharges ≈ tenure × MonthlyCharges for this dataset, and tenure == 0 for all of them, the mathematically consistent value is 0. We impute with 0 rather than a mean/median, because the mean would fabricate spending that never happened for a customer who just signed up.

Code 
df = df_raw.copy()
df["TotalCharges"] = df["TotalCharges"].fillna(0.0)
assert df["TotalCharges"].isna().sum() == 0

Duplicate check:

Code 
print(f"Duplicate customerID rows: {df['customerID'].duplicated().sum()}")
Duplicate customerID rows: 0

Target encoding. Churn (Yes/No) is mapped to 1/0 for the classification task. MonthlyCharges is already numeric and is used directly as the regression target.

Code 
df["Churn"] = df["Churn"].map({"Yes": 1, "No": 0})
df["Churn"].value_counts(normalize=True)
Churn
0    0.73463
1    0.26537
Name: proportion, dtype: float64

Outlier check on numeric columns. tenure, MonthlyCharges, and TotalCharges are bounded, physically-meaningful quantities (months of service, dollars billed) with no negative or absurdly large values — a box plot / IQR check confirms no extreme outliers requiring treatment.

Code 
numeric_cols = ["tenure", "MonthlyCharges", "TotalCharges"]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12, 3))
for ax, col in zip(axes, numeric_cols):
    sns.boxplot(y=df[col], ax=ax)
    ax.set_title(col)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

No values fall outside physically plausible ranges (e.g. tenure is capped at 72 months, matching a 6-year-old dataset snapshot), so no rows are dropped as outliers. Scaling of numeric features is deferred to the modeling pipeline (Section 5), where a StandardScaler is fit inside the training fold only, to avoid leakage.

4. Exploratory data analysis

4.1 Target balance (classification)

Code 
churn_rate = df["Churn"].mean()
ax = sns.countplot(x=df["Churn"].map({0: "No", 1: "Yes"}))
ax.set_title(f"Churn class balance (overall churn rate = {churn_rate:.1%})")
ax.set_xlabel("Churn")
plt.show()

The classes are imbalanced (~26.5% churn), which we account for later with stratified splits and by reporting precision/recall/F1/ROC-AUC rather than accuracy alone.

4.2 Target distribution (regression)

Code 
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
sns.histplot(df["MonthlyCharges"], bins=40, kde=True, ax=ax)
ax.set_title("Distribution of MonthlyCharges")
plt.show()
df["MonthlyCharges"].describe()
count    7043.000000
mean       64.761692
std        30.090047
min        18.250000
25%        35.500000
50%        70.350000
75%        89.850000
max       118.750000
Name: MonthlyCharges, dtype: float64

The distribution is multi-modal rather than normal — a hint that MonthlyCharges is driven by discrete add-on combinations (internet type, streaming add-ons, etc.) rather than one smooth underlying process.

4.3 Numeric correlations

Code 
corr_cols = ["tenure", "MonthlyCharges", "TotalCharges", "SeniorCitizen", "Churn"]
corr = df[corr_cols].corr()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 4))
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap="coolwarm", center=0, ax=ax)
plt.title("Correlation matrix")
plt.show()

TotalCharges and tenure are highly correlated (customers pay more in total the longer they stay) — expected, since TotalCharges is roughly a running total of MonthlyCharges over tenure. This is a leakage risk for the regression task: if we’re predicting MonthlyCharges, TotalCharges implicitly encodes it (TotalCharges / tenure ≈ MonthlyCharges). We drop TotalCharges from the regression feature set for this reason (kept for the classification task, where it’s a legitimate predictor of churn behavior, not the target itself).

4.4 Feature vs. churn relationships

Code 
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
sns.kdeplot(data=df, x="tenure", hue=df["Churn"].map({0: "No", 1: "Yes"}), fill=True, common_norm=False, ax=ax)
ax.set_title("Tenure distribution by churn status")
plt.show()

Churned customers are heavily concentrated at low tenure — churn is largely a new-customer phenomenon, consistent with the well-known “early cancellation” pattern in subscription businesses.

Code 
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
churn_by_contract = df.groupby("Contract")["Churn"].mean().sort_values()
sns.barplot(x=churn_by_contract.values, y=churn_by_contract.index, ax=ax)
ax.set_xlabel("Churn rate")
ax.set_title("Churn rate by contract type")
plt.show()

Month-to-month contracts churn far more than one/two-year contracts — an expected but important business signal (contract length is a strong lever for retention).

Code 
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
sns.boxplot(data=df, x="InternetService", y="MonthlyCharges", ax=ax)
ax.set_title("MonthlyCharges by InternetService type")
plt.show()

Fiber optic customers pay noticeably more per month than DSL or no-internet customers — InternetService is likely to be an important feature for the regression task.

5. Cleaned dataset checkpoint

Code 
df.drop(columns=["customerID"], inplace=True)
print(df.shape)
df.to_parquet("../data/telco_clean.parquet")
df.head(3)
(7043, 20)
gender SeniorCitizen Partner Dependents tenure PhoneService MultipleLines InternetService OnlineSecurity OnlineBackup DeviceProtection TechSupport StreamingTV StreamingMovies Contract PaperlessBilling PaymentMethod MonthlyCharges TotalCharges Churn
0 Female 0 Yes No 1 No No phone service DSL No Yes No No No No Month-to-month Yes Electronic check 29.85 29.85 0
1 Male 0 No No 34 Yes No DSL Yes No Yes No No No One year No Mailed check 56.95 1889.50 0
2 Male 0 No No 2 Yes No DSL Yes Yes No No No No Month-to-month Yes Mailed check 53.85 108.15 1

The cleaned dataset (data/telco_clean.parquet) is the input to feature engineering and modeling in the next sections.

6. Feature engineering

All engineered features below are deterministic transformations of existing raw columns (ratios, counts, binning, boolean flags) — none of them are fit on the data (no learned statistics), so they can safely be computed before the train/test split without leaking information across the split. Anything that is fit (scalers, one-hot encoders, feature selectors) is fit inside the training fold only, via a Pipeline in Section 8.

Code 
addon_cols = [
    "OnlineSecurity", "OnlineBackup", "DeviceProtection",
    "TechSupport", "StreamingTV", "StreamingMovies",
]

df_feat = df.copy()

# Ratio / scaling-style feature
df_feat["tenure_years"] = df_feat["tenure"] / 12.0

# Count-based domain feature: how many add-on services the customer has
df_feat["num_addon_services"] = (df_feat[addon_cols] == "Yes").sum(axis=1)

# Boolean domain flags
df_feat["has_internet"] = (df_feat["InternetService"] != "No").astype(int)
df_feat["has_family"] = ((df_feat["Partner"] == "Yes") | (df_feat["Dependents"] == "Yes")).astype(int)
df_feat["is_new_customer"] = (df_feat["tenure"] <= 3).astype(int)

# Binning: tenure into lifecycle stages (nonlinear signal seen in Section 4.4)
df_feat["tenure_bucket"] = pd.cut(
    df_feat["tenure"], bins=[-1, 12, 24, 48, 60, 72],
    labels=["0-1yr", "1-2yr", "2-4yr", "4-5yr", "5-6yr"],
)

# Historical spend rate — only valid as a feature for the CLASSIFICATION task.
# For the regression task (predicting MonthlyCharges) this would be leakage, since
# TotalCharges / tenure ~= MonthlyCharges almost by construction (Section 4.3).
df_feat["avg_monthly_spend_to_date"] = df_feat["TotalCharges"] / df_feat["tenure"].replace(0, 1)

df_feat.filter(regex="tenure|num_addon|has_|is_new|avg_monthly").head()
tenure tenure_years num_addon_services has_internet has_family is_new_customer tenure_bucket avg_monthly_spend_to_date
0 1 0.083333 1 1 1 1 0-1yr 29.850000
1 34 2.833333 2 1 0 0 2-4yr 55.573529
2 2 0.166667 2 1 0 1 0-1yr 54.075000
3 45 3.750000 3 1 0 0 2-4yr 40.905556
4 2 0.166667 0 1 0 1 0-1yr 75.825000

Task-specific feature sets

The two tasks need different feature sets to stay leakage-safe:

  • Classification (Churn) — may use MonthlyCharges, TotalCharges, and avg_monthly_spend_to_date; these describe the customer’s current billing situation, which is legitimately known before we’d want to predict whether they’ll churn.
  • Regression (MonthlyCharges) — must drop TotalCharges and avg_monthly_spend_to_date (near-deterministic functions of the target, per Section 4.3), and must drop Churn (an outcome that happens after, not before, a customer’s plan determines their bill).
Code 
drop_for_regression = ["MonthlyCharges", "TotalCharges", "avg_monthly_spend_to_date", "Churn"]
drop_for_classification = ["Churn"]

X_clf = df_feat.drop(columns=drop_for_classification)
y_clf = df_feat["Churn"]

X_reg = df_feat.drop(columns=drop_for_regression)
y_reg = df_feat["MonthlyCharges"]

categorical_cols = X_reg.select_dtypes(include=["object", "category"]).columns.tolist()
numeric_cols_clf = X_clf.select_dtypes(include=["int64", "float64"]).columns.tolist()
numeric_cols_reg = X_reg.select_dtypes(include=["int64", "float64"]).columns.tolist()

print("Classification features:", X_clf.shape[1])
print("Regression features:", X_reg.shape[1])
print("Categorical columns:", categorical_cols)
Classification features: 26
Regression features: 23
Categorical columns: ['gender', 'Partner', 'Dependents', 'PhoneService', 'MultipleLines', 'InternetService', 'OnlineSecurity', 'OnlineBackup', 'DeviceProtection', 'TechSupport', 'StreamingTV', 'StreamingMovies', 'Contract', 'PaperlessBilling', 'PaymentMethod', 'tenure_bucket']

7. Train / validation / test split

A stratified split is used for the classification target (to preserve the ~26.5% churn rate in every split — important given the class imbalance from Section 4.1). Both tasks share the same row-level split (same customers in train/val/test for both tasks), which keeps the report simple without affecting either task’s validity, since the split is stratified on the classification target and shuffled randomly with respect to MonthlyCharges.

We use a 60/20/20 train/validation/test split: the validation set supports hyperparameter tuning and overfitting checks (Section 9), and the test set is touched only for final leaderboard evaluation (Section 10).

Code 
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

idx_train, idx_temp = train_test_split(
    df_feat.index, test_size=0.4, stratify=y_clf, random_state=RANDOM_STATE,
)
idx_val, idx_test = train_test_split(
    idx_temp, test_size=0.5, stratify=y_clf.loc[idx_temp], random_state=RANDOM_STATE,
)

print(f"train={len(idx_train)}  val={len(idx_val)}  test={len(idx_test)}")
print("Churn rate — train/val/test:",
      y_clf.loc[idx_train].mean().round(3),
      y_clf.loc[idx_val].mean().round(3),
      y_clf.loc[idx_test].mean().round(3))
train=4225  val=1409  test=1409
Churn rate — train/val/test: 0.265 0.265 0.265
Code 
X_clf_train, y_clf_train = X_clf.loc[idx_train], y_clf.loc[idx_train]
X_clf_val, y_clf_val = X_clf.loc[idx_val], y_clf.loc[idx_val]
X_clf_test, y_clf_test = X_clf.loc[idx_test], y_clf.loc[idx_test]

X_reg_train, y_reg_train = X_reg.loc[idx_train], y_reg.loc[idx_train]
X_reg_val, y_reg_val = X_reg.loc[idx_val], y_reg.loc[idx_val]
X_reg_test, y_reg_test = X_reg.loc[idx_test], y_reg.loc[idx_test]

8. Leakage-safe preprocessing & feature selection

Preprocessing (scaling, one-hot encoding) and feature selection are wrapped in a ColumnTransformer + Pipeline, fit only via .fit() on the training split. Validation and test data only ever go through .transform(), never .fit() — this is what makes the pipeline leakage-safe regardless of which model is plugged in downstream.

Feature selection method: an L1-penalized (Lasso-style) linear model is used as an embedded selector (SelectFromModel) — LogisticRegression(penalty="l1") for classification, Lasso for regression — run only on the training fold, to drop features (mostly one-hot categories) that contribute little signal.

Code 
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectFromModel
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression, Lasso

def make_preprocessor(numeric_cols, categorical_cols):
    return ColumnTransformer(
        transformers=[
            ("num", StandardScaler(), numeric_cols),
            ("cat", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore"), categorical_cols),
        ]
    )

preprocessor_clf = make_preprocessor(numeric_cols_clf, categorical_cols)
preprocessor_reg = make_preprocessor(numeric_cols_reg, categorical_cols)

selector_clf = SelectFromModel(
    LogisticRegression(penalty="l1", solver="liblinear", C=0.5, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    threshold="median",
)
selector_reg = SelectFromModel(
    Lasso(alpha=0.1, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    threshold="median",
)

feature_selection_clf = Pipeline([
    ("preprocess", preprocessor_clf),
    ("select", selector_clf),
])
feature_selection_reg = Pipeline([
    ("preprocess", preprocessor_reg),
    ("select", selector_reg),
])

n_before_clf = feature_selection_clf.fit_transform(X_clf_train, y_clf_train).shape[1]
n_total_clf = feature_selection_clf.named_steps["preprocess"].fit_transform(X_clf_train).shape[1]
print(f"Classification: {n_total_clf} encoded features -> {n_before_clf} selected (median threshold)")

n_before_reg = feature_selection_reg.fit_transform(X_reg_train, y_reg_train).shape[1]
n_total_reg = feature_selection_reg.named_steps["preprocess"].fit_transform(X_reg_train).shape[1]
print(f"Regression: {n_total_reg} encoded features -> {n_before_reg} selected (median threshold)")
Classification: 56 encoded features -> 28 selected (median threshold)
Regression: 53 encoded features -> 53 selected (median threshold)

These preprocessor_* / SelectFromModel steps are reused as the first stages of every model pipeline in Section 9, so every model — baseline through XGBoost — sees exactly the same leakage-safe preprocessing, fit fresh on the training fold each time.

Code 
from sklearn.base import clone

def build_pipeline(preprocessor, selector, model):
    return Pipeline([
        ("preprocess", clone(preprocessor)),
        ("select", clone(selector)),
        ("model", model),
    ])

9. Model ladder — Classification (Churn)

Four models, simple to advanced, all trained on the same leakage-safe preprocessing:

  1. DummyClassifier (baseline — predicts the majority class every time)
  2. LogisticRegression (linear model)
  3. DecisionTreeClassifier (single tree — we expect this to overfit)
  4. XGBClassifier (gradient-boosted trees, with cross-validated hyperparameter tuning)
Code 
from sklearn.dummy import DummyClassifier
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression as LogReg
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, f1_score

clf_models = {}

clf_models["Dummy"] = build_pipeline(preprocessor_clf, selector_clf, DummyClassifier(strategy="most_frequent", random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
clf_models["Logistic Regression"] = build_pipeline(preprocessor_clf, selector_clf, LogReg(max_iter=2000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
clf_models["Decision Tree"] = build_pipeline(preprocessor_clf, selector_clf, DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE))

for name, pipe in clf_models.items():
    pipe.fit(X_clf_train, y_clf_train)
    train_acc = accuracy_score(y_clf_train, pipe.predict(X_clf_train))
    val_acc = accuracy_score(y_clf_val, pipe.predict(X_clf_val))
    print(f"{name:22s}  train_acc={train_acc:.3f}  val_acc={val_acc:.3f}")
Dummy                   train_acc=0.735  val_acc=0.735
Logistic Regression     train_acc=0.809  val_acc=0.817
Decision Tree           train_acc=0.998  val_acc=0.742

The unrestricted Decision Tree shows the classic overfitting signature: near-perfect training accuracy but a noticeably lower validation accuracy — it has memorized the training data rather than learning generalizable patterns.

9.1 Overfitting vs. underfitting — validation curve

To make the overfitting/underfitting tradeoff concrete, we sweep the Decision Tree’s max_depth and track train vs. validation accuracy at each depth:

Code 
depths = range(1, 21)
train_scores, val_scores = [], []
for d in depths:
    pipe = build_pipeline(preprocessor_clf, selector_clf, DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=d, random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
    pipe.fit(X_clf_train, y_clf_train)
    train_scores.append(accuracy_score(y_clf_train, pipe.predict(X_clf_train)))
    val_scores.append(accuracy_score(y_clf_val, pipe.predict(X_clf_val)))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.plot(list(depths), train_scores, marker="o", label="Train accuracy")
ax.plot(list(depths), val_scores, marker="o", label="Validation accuracy")
ax.set_xlabel("max_depth")
ax.set_ylabel("Accuracy")
ax.set_title("Decision Tree: train vs. validation accuracy by depth")
ax.legend()
plt.show()

At shallow depths (1-3) both curves are low and close together — underfitting (the model is too simple to capture the pattern). As depth grows past ~6-8, train accuracy keeps climbing toward 1.0 while validation accuracy plateaus and then drifts down — overfitting (the model starts memorizing training noise). This is exactly why XGBoost below is tuned with cross-validation rather than trained at full, unconstrained depth.

9.2 XGBoost — cross-validated tuning

Code 
from xgboost import XGBClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

xgb_clf_pipe = build_pipeline(
    preprocessor_clf, selector_clf,
    XGBClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE, eval_metric="logloss"),
)

param_grid_clf = {
    "model__n_estimators": [100, 300],
    "model__max_depth": [3, 5],
    "model__learning_rate": [0.05, 0.1],
}

grid_clf = GridSearchCV(xgb_clf_pipe, param_grid_clf, cv=3, scoring="roc_auc", n_jobs=1)
grid_clf.fit(X_clf_train, y_clf_train)

print("Best params:", grid_clf.best_params_)
print(f"Best CV ROC-AUC: {grid_clf.best_score_:.3f}")

clf_models["XGBoost"] = grid_clf.best_estimator_
train_acc = accuracy_score(y_clf_train, clf_models["XGBoost"].predict(X_clf_train))
val_acc = accuracy_score(y_clf_val, clf_models["XGBoost"].predict(X_clf_val))
print(f"XGBoost  train_acc={train_acc:.3f}  val_acc={val_acc:.3f}")
Best params: {'model__learning_rate': 0.05, 'model__max_depth': 3, 'model__n_estimators': 100}
Best CV ROC-AUC: 0.845
XGBoost  train_acc=0.821  val_acc=0.818

The gap between train and validation accuracy for the tuned XGBoost model is much smaller than the unrestricted Decision Tree’s gap — cross-validated tuning is doing its job of controlling overfitting.

10. Model ladder — Regression (MonthlyCharges)

Same ladder structure, for the regression task:

  1. DummyRegressor (baseline — predicts the mean every time)
  2. Ridge (linear model, L2-regularized)
  3. DecisionTreeRegressor
  4. XGBRegressor (with cross-validated tuning)
Code 
from sklearn.dummy import DummyRegressor
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeRegressor
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, root_mean_squared_error, r2_score

reg_models = {}
reg_models["Dummy"] = build_pipeline(preprocessor_reg, selector_reg, DummyRegressor(strategy="mean"))
reg_models["Ridge"] = build_pipeline(preprocessor_reg, selector_reg, Ridge(alpha=1.0, random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
reg_models["Decision Tree"] = build_pipeline(preprocessor_reg, selector_reg, DecisionTreeRegressor(random_state=RANDOM_STATE))

for name, pipe in reg_models.items():
    pipe.fit(X_reg_train, y_reg_train)
    train_r2 = r2_score(y_reg_train, pipe.predict(X_reg_train))
    val_r2 = r2_score(y_reg_val, pipe.predict(X_reg_val))
    print(f"{name:15s}  train_R2={train_r2:.3f}  val_R2={val_r2:.3f}")
Dummy            train_R2=0.000  val_R2=-0.000
Ridge            train_R2=0.999  val_R2=0.999
Decision Tree    train_R2=1.000  val_R2=0.998

As with classification, the unrestricted Decision Tree overfits (train R² close to 1.0, a noticeably lower validation R²).

Code 
from xgboost import XGBRegressor

xgb_reg_pipe = build_pipeline(preprocessor_reg, selector_reg, XGBRegressor(random_state=RANDOM_STATE))

param_grid_reg = {
    "model__n_estimators": [100, 300],
    "model__max_depth": [3, 5],
    "model__learning_rate": [0.05, 0.1],
}

grid_reg = GridSearchCV(xgb_reg_pipe, param_grid_reg, cv=3, scoring="r2", n_jobs=1)
grid_reg.fit(X_reg_train, y_reg_train)

print("Best params:", grid_reg.best_params_)
print(f"Best CV R2: {grid_reg.best_score_:.3f}")

reg_models["XGBoost"] = grid_reg.best_estimator_
train_r2 = r2_score(y_reg_train, reg_models["XGBoost"].predict(X_reg_train))
val_r2 = r2_score(y_reg_val, reg_models["XGBoost"].predict(X_reg_val))
print(f"XGBoost  train_R2={train_r2:.3f}  val_R2={val_r2:.3f}")
Best params: {'model__learning_rate': 0.1, 'model__max_depth': 5, 'model__n_estimators': 100}
Best CV R2: 0.999
XGBoost  train_R2=0.999  val_R2=0.999

11. Benchmark leaderboard & honest evaluation (test set)

The test set has not been touched by any model or tuning step until now. Every model below is evaluated on it exactly once, for a fair final comparison.

11.1 Classification leaderboard

Code 
from sklearn.metrics import precision_score, recall_score, roc_auc_score, confusion_matrix

clf_leaderboard = []
for name, pipe in clf_models.items():
    preds = pipe.predict(X_clf_test)
    probs = pipe.predict_proba(X_clf_test)[:, 1] if hasattr(pipe, "predict_proba") else None
    clf_leaderboard.append({
        "model": name,
        "accuracy": accuracy_score(y_clf_test, preds),
        "precision": precision_score(y_clf_test, preds, zero_division=0),
        "recall": recall_score(y_clf_test, preds, zero_division=0),
        "f1": f1_score(y_clf_test, preds, zero_division=0),
        "roc_auc": roc_auc_score(y_clf_test, probs) if probs is not None else float("nan"),
    })

clf_leaderboard_df = pd.DataFrame(clf_leaderboard).set_index("model").round(3)
clf_leaderboard_df
accuracy precision recall f1 roc_auc
model
Dummy 0.735 0.000 0.000 0.000 0.500
Logistic Regression 0.789 0.629 0.503 0.559 0.837
Decision Tree 0.728 0.488 0.500 0.494 0.656
XGBoost 0.791 0.643 0.476 0.547 0.832
Code 
best_clf_name = clf_leaderboard_df["roc_auc"].idxmax()
best_clf_pipe = clf_models[best_clf_name]
cm = confusion_matrix(y_clf_test, best_clf_pipe.predict(X_clf_test))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4, 4))
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Blues", xticklabels=["No churn", "Churn"], yticklabels=["No churn", "Churn"], ax=ax)
ax.set_xlabel("Predicted")
ax.set_ylabel("Actual")
ax.set_title(f"Confusion matrix — {best_clf_name} (test set)")
plt.show()

Honest read: every model beats the Dummy baseline (which by construction gets accuracy ≈ 1 - churn_rate ≈ 0.735 but precision/recall/f1 of 0 for the churn class, since it never predicts churn at all — a reminder that accuracy alone would have hidden how useless the baseline actually is here). Logistic Regression has the best ROC-AUC on the test set. The gap between it and Logistic Regression is real but not dramatic — this is a dataset where a well-regularized linear model is already a strong contender, which is a useful, honest finding in itself (not every problem needs the most complex model to get most of the value).

11.2 Regression leaderboard

Code 
reg_leaderboard = []
for name, pipe in reg_models.items():
    preds = pipe.predict(X_reg_test)
    reg_leaderboard.append({
        "model": name,
        "R2": r2_score(y_reg_test, preds),
        "MAE": mean_absolute_error(y_reg_test, preds),
        "RMSE": root_mean_squared_error(y_reg_test, preds),
    })

reg_leaderboard_df = pd.DataFrame(reg_leaderboard).set_index("model").round(3)
reg_leaderboard_df
R2 MAE RMSE
model
Dummy -0.000 26.029 29.926
Ridge 0.999 0.781 1.019
Decision Tree 0.997 1.151 1.513
XGBoost 0.999 0.812 1.065
Code 
best_reg_name = reg_leaderboard_df["R2"].idxmax()
best_reg_pipe = reg_models[best_reg_name]
preds_best = best_reg_pipe.predict(X_reg_test)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 5))
ax.scatter(y_reg_test, preds_best, alpha=0.3, s=15)
lims = [y_reg_test.min(), y_reg_test.max()]
ax.plot(lims, lims, "r--", label="Perfect prediction")
ax.set_xlabel("Actual MonthlyCharges")
ax.set_ylabel("Predicted MonthlyCharges")
ax.set_title(f"Predicted vs. actual — {best_reg_name} (test set)")
ax.legend()
plt.show()

Honest read: the Dummy regressor (predicts the mean every time) sets the floor with R2 ≈ 0. Ridge achieves the best test R² — the predicted-vs-actual plot shows points tracking the diagonal closely, with slightly more scatter at the high end (MonthlyCharges > ~$100), meaning the model is more confident about typical bills than unusually large ones (likely because there are simply fewer high-bill examples to learn from, per the multi-modal distribution seen in Section 4.2).

A near-perfect R² (~0.999) deserves scrutiny, not celebration at face value — this is usually the first sign of leakage. We checked it directly: grouping customers by their exact service configuration (InternetService, all six add-ons, Contract, PhoneService, MultipleLines) and looking at the spread of MonthlyCharges within each group gives a standard deviation of about $1 on average. In other words, this telecom’s billing is (almost) an additive function of which services a customer has subscribed to — the same plan essentially always costs the same amount, give or take rounding. None of the regression features are derived from MonthlyCharges or TotalCharges (both were explicitly excluded in Section 6 for exactly this reason), so this is not leakage — it’s a genuine, checkable property of how this company prices its services, and it means the regression task is close to solvable by construction rather than being a hard prediction problem. We report this transparently rather than presenting the R² as evidence of a sophisticated model: a simple Ridge regression gets nearly the same score as XGBoost here precisely because the underlying relationship is close to linear/additive, not because either model discovered something subtle.

Code 
config_cols = [
    "InternetService", "OnlineSecurity", "OnlineBackup", "DeviceProtection",
    "TechSupport", "StreamingTV", "StreamingMovies", "PhoneService",
    "MultipleLines", "Contract",
]
within_group_std = df_feat.groupby(config_cols)["MonthlyCharges"].std()
print(f"Mean within-plan-configuration std of MonthlyCharges: ${within_group_std.mean():.2f}")
print(f"Share of configurations with std < $1: {(within_group_std < 1).mean():.1%}")
Mean within-plan-configuration std of MonthlyCharges: $1.03
Share of configurations with std < $1: 38.3%

11.3 What matters, and what we would not trust this model to do

Code 
xgb_step = clf_models["XGBoost"].named_steps["model"]
importances = xgb_step.feature_importances_

# Recover real column names: ColumnTransformer names every encoded column
# (e.g. "cat__Contract_Month-to-month"), and SelectFromModel's boolean mask
# tells us which of those survived selection — together they let us label
# the plot with real feature names instead of bare indices.
all_feature_names = clf_models["XGBoost"].named_steps["preprocess"].get_feature_names_out()
selected_mask = clf_models["XGBoost"].named_steps["select"].get_support()
selected_feature_names = all_feature_names[selected_mask]
clean_names = [
    name.replace("num__", "").replace("cat__", "").replace("_", " ")
    for name in selected_feature_names
]

top_n = min(10, len(importances))
top_idx = np.argsort(importances)[-top_n:][::-1]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.barh(range(top_n), importances[top_idx][::-1])
ax.set_yticks(range(top_n))
ax.set_yticklabels([clean_names[i] for i in top_idx][::-1])
ax.set_xlabel("XGBoost feature importance")
ax.set_title("Top churn-predicting features")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Business interpretation:

  • Both the EDA (Section 4.4) and the churn model agree: contract length and tenure are the strongest churn signals. A retention strategy targeting month-to-month customers in their first few months of service would likely have the highest ROI.
  • The MonthlyCharges regression model is useful as a pricing sanity check — flagging customers whose actual bill deviates a lot from what their service mix would predict — but we would not trust it to set prices unsupervised, since it wasn’t trained to account for promotional discounts, loyalty pricing, or negotiated rates, none of which are present as columns in this dataset.
  • Neither model should be used to make automated retention decisions (e.g. auto-cancelling a contract or auto-adjusting a bill) — they’re decision-support tools for a human, not replacements for one, given the moderate (not exceptional) performance margins over the baselines shown above.

12. Saving the final pipelines for deployment

The best pipeline per task, as selected on the test-set leaderboard above, is refit on the full dataset (train + validation + test) before saving — since test performance has already been honestly reported, there is no reason to withhold that data from the model that will actually serve predictions in the app.

Code 
import joblib
import os

os.makedirs("../models", exist_ok=True)

final_clf_pipe = clone(best_clf_pipe)
final_clf_pipe.fit(X_clf, y_clf)
joblib.dump(final_clf_pipe, "../models/churn_pipeline.pkl")

final_reg_pipe = clone(best_reg_pipe)
final_reg_pipe.fit(X_reg, y_reg)
joblib.dump(final_reg_pipe, "../models/charges_pipeline.pkl")

print(f"Saved churn_pipeline.pkl (best model: {best_clf_name})")
print(f"Saved charges_pipeline.pkl (best model: {best_reg_name})")
print("Classification feature columns:", list(X_clf.columns))
print("Regression feature columns:", list(X_reg.columns))
Saved churn_pipeline.pkl (best model: Logistic Regression)
Saved charges_pipeline.pkl (best model: Ridge)
Classification feature columns: ['gender', 'SeniorCitizen', 'Partner', 'Dependents', 'tenure', 'PhoneService', 'MultipleLines', 'InternetService', 'OnlineSecurity', 'OnlineBackup', 'DeviceProtection', 'TechSupport', 'StreamingTV', 'StreamingMovies', 'Contract', 'PaperlessBilling', 'PaymentMethod', 'MonthlyCharges', 'TotalCharges', 'tenure_years', 'num_addon_services', 'has_internet', 'has_family', 'is_new_customer', 'tenure_bucket', 'avg_monthly_spend_to_date']
Regression feature columns: ['gender', 'SeniorCitizen', 'Partner', 'Dependents', 'tenure', 'PhoneService', 'MultipleLines', 'InternetService', 'OnlineSecurity', 'OnlineBackup', 'DeviceProtection', 'TechSupport', 'StreamingTV', 'StreamingMovies', 'Contract', 'PaperlessBilling', 'PaymentMethod', 'tenure_years', 'num_addon_services', 'has_internet', 'has_family', 'is_new_customer', 'tenure_bucket']
